text.title: Usability Testing Process
text.summary: Seven-step process for planning and conducting usability tests that produce actionable findings.

rule.statement: State what you want to learn before designing tasks or recruiting participants.
rule.rationale: Goals determine task design, participant criteria, and what metrics to collect. Without clear goals, sessions drift and findings are unfocused.

example.note: Goal examples: Can users complete checkout in under 3 minutes? Do users understand the navigation structure? What blocks users from signing up?

section.title: Step 2 — Create Test Plan

rule.statement: Document objectives, participant profile, tasks, metrics, and test environment before scheduling any sessions.
rule.rationale: A written plan aligns stakeholders, prevents scope creep during sessions, and creates an audit trail for findings.

section.reference: Full test plan template with all required sections

rule.statement: Write tasks as realistic scenarios with a clear goal and no hints about the solution path.
rule.rationale: Task wording that hints at the solution (mentioning button names or menu paths) invalidates the test by guiding the user to the answer.

example.note: Scenario: You need to change your delivery address for an upcoming order. Task: Find your recent order and update the shipping address. Success: User locates order and changes address.
"Click the account button and select orders" — gives away the answer. Use "Find your order history" instead.

rule.statement: Recruit 5-8 participants per user segment who match the target user profile.
rule.rationale: Nielsen's research shows 5 participants reveal 85% of usability problems. Below 3, pattern detection is meaningless. Mix experience levels to surface both novice and expert issues.

section.title: Step 5 — Conduct Sessions

rule.statement: Follow a consistent session structure: intro (5 min), pre-test questions (5 min), tasks (30-45 min), post-test questions (10 min), debrief (5 min).
rule.rationale: Consistent structure enables cross-session comparison and ensures participants understand the think-aloud protocol before tasks begin.

rule.statement: Do not help participants during tasks, even when they are visibly struggling.
rule.rationale: Helping invalidates the observation. Struggling is data — it shows where the design fails.

section.reference: Facilitator script with exact wording for each session phase

rule.statement: Group observations by task, rate severity by frequency and task-blocking impact, then prioritize findings.
rule.rationale: Severity rating directs engineering effort to problems with the highest user impact before those that are merely annoying.

section.title: Step 7 — Report and Act

rule.statement: Each finding must include: severity, affected task, frequency (N of total participants), observed problem, evidence, and a concrete recommendation.
rule.rationale: Findings without evidence are dismissed. Findings without recommendations create discussion but no action.

section.reference: Finding format template with severity reference table
